content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

November 3, 1997

Robert W. Ford, Chair
CDF Firefighters PAC
924 Enterprise Drive
Sacramento, California 95825

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File Nos. A-97-124 and A-97-363

Dear Mr. Ford:

This letter is in response to both of your requests for advice on behalf of the California
Department of Firefighters regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS

1. The statewide organization of the California Department of Forestry ("CDF")
Firefighters has established a general purpose PAC. May this PAC make both contributions and
independent expenditures?

Yes, the CDF Firefighters' general purpose PAC may make both contributions and
independent expenditures. In most circumstances, Proposition 208 does limit the amount of
independent expenditures that can be made to support or oppose a candidate. However, Section
85500(c),² regarding independent expenditures, states that:

"(c) Any contributor that makes a contribution of one hundred
dollars ($100) or more per election to a candidate for elective
office shall be considered to be acting in concert with that
candidate and shall not make independent expenditures and
contributions which in combination exceed the amounts set

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² You may be aware that portions of Proposition 208 are the subject of litigation pending in the United
States District Court, Eastern District of California entitled, California Prolife Council Political Action Committee v.
FPPC, et al. Case No. CIV-S-96-1965-LKK. Section 85500 is being challenged in that lawsuit.

428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, CA 95814●P. O. Box 807, Sacramento, CA 95812● (916) 322-5660

A-97-124 and A-97-363
Page 2

forth in Section 85301 in support of that candidate or in
opposition to that candidate's opponent or opponents."

A committee that makes independent expenditures of $1,000 or more supporting or
opposing a candidate shall not accept any contribution in excess of $250 per election.
(Section 85500(b).)

Under Section 85500(d), an expenditure shall not be considered independent and shall be
treated as a contribution when the expenditure is made either:

"(1) With the cooperation of, or in consultation with, any candidate or
any authorized committee or agent of the candidate.
(2) In concert with, or at the request or suggestion of, any
candidate or any authorized committee or agent of the candidate.
(3) Under any arrangement, coordination, or direction with
respect to the candidate or the candidate's agent and the person
making the expenditure.
(4) By a candidate or officeholder supporting another candidate
or officeholder of the same political party running for a seat in the
same legislative body of the candidate or officeholder."

2. May a general purpose PAC make contributions to a candidate's campaign committee
and also make independent expenditures supporting other candidates who are running for a
different seat or elective office and who have not received a contribution from the general
purpose PAC?

The general purpose PAC may make contributions of $100 or more per election to a
candidate's campaign committee, in accordance with the applicable contribution limits, and also
make independent expenditures supporting other candidates who are running for a different
elective office. If the general purpose PAC makes an independent expenditure of $1,000 or more
to any candidate, Section 85500(b), quoted above, applies.

3. May a majority of the directors/officers of the CDF Firefighters' general purpose
PAC also be the directors/officers of an independent expenditure PAC?

Yes, the two committees may have a majority of the same officers/directors. (Patack
Advice Letter, No. A-97-441, copy enclosed.) Pursuant to Regulation 18531.1(d)(2),³
entities
that have the same, or a majority of the same, persons as board members or principal officers are
considered "affiliated" for the purpose of sharing one contribution limit.

³ Regulation 18531.1: Affiliated Entities Sharing One Contribution Limit, was adopted by the
Commission on September 18, 1997. A copy of this regulation is enclosed.

A-97-124 and A-97-363
Page 3

However, since only contributions are aggregated for purposes of Regulation 18531.1,
this normally will not be of consequence for the two committees. The one exception would be if
the general purpose PAC gives a candidate a contribution of $100 or more, then the prohibition
in Section 85500(c) will apply to both the general purpose PAC and the independent expenditure
committee.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5560.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jill Stecher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:tls
Enclosures

S AND CONCLUSIONS
1. The statewide organization ofthe California Department of Forestiy (“CDF”)
Firefighters has established a generalpurpose FAG. May this PAC make both contributions and
independent expenditures?
Yes, the CDF Firefighters’ general purpose PAC may make both contributions and
independent expenditures. In most circumstances, Proposition 208 does not limit the amount of
independent expenditures that can be made to support or oppose a candidate. However, Section
85500(c),
2 regarding independent expenditures, states that:
“(c) Any contributor that makes a contribution of one hundred
dollars ($100) or more per election to a candidate for elective
office shall be considered to be acting in concert with that
candidate and shall not make independent expenditures and
contributions which in combination exceed the amounts set
2 You may be aware that portions of Proposition 208 are the subject of litigation pending in the United
States District Court, Eastern District of California entitled, California Prolfe Council Political Action Committee v.
FPPC, eta!. Case No. CW-S-96-1965-LKK. Section 85500 is being challenged in that lawsuit.
. (916) 322-5660


A-97-124 and A-97-363
forth in Section 85301 in support of that candidate or in
opposition to that candidate’s opponent or opponents.”
A committee that makes independent expenditures of $1,000 or more supporting or
opposing a candidate shall not accept any contribution in excess of $250 per election.
(Section 85500(b).)
Under Section 85500(d), an expenditure shall not be considered independent and shall be
treated as a contribution when the expenditure is made either:
“(1) With the cooperation of, or in consultation with, any candidate or
any authorized committee or agent ofthe candidate.
(2) In concert with, or at the request or suggestion of, any
candidate or any authorized committee or agent of the candidate.
(3) Under any arrangement, coordination, or direction with
respect to the candidate or the candidate’s agent and the person
making the expenditure.
(4) By a candidate or officeholder supporting another candidate
or officeholder of the same political party running for a seat in the
same legislative body of the candidate or officeholder.”
2. May a generalpurpose PAC make contributions to a candidate’s campaign committee
and also make independent expenditures supporting other candidates who are runningfor a
different seat or elective office and who have not received a contribution from the general
purpose PAC?
The general purpose PAC may make contributions of $100 or more per election to a
candidate’s campaign committee, in accordance with the applicable contribution limits, and also
make independent expenditures supporting other candidates who are running for a different
elective office. If the general purpose PAC makes an independent expenditure of $1,000 or more
to any candidate, Section 85500(b), quoted above, applies.
3. May a majority ofthe directors/officers ofthe CDFFirefighters’ generalpurpose
PAC also be the directors/officers ofan independent expenditure PAC?
Yes, the two committees may have a majority ofthe same officers/directors. (Patack
Advice Letter, No. A-97-441, copy enclosed.) Pursuant to Regulation 18531.1 (d)(2),
3 entities
that have the same, or a majority of the same, persons as board members or principal officers are
considered “affiliated” for the purpose of sharing one contribution limit.
Regulation 18531.1: Affiliated Entities Sharing One Contribution Limit, was adopted by the
Commission on September 18, 1997. A copy of this regulation is enclosed.


A-97-124 and A-97-363
However, since only contributions are aggregated for purposes of Regulation 18531.1,
this normally will not be of consequence for the two committees. The one exception would be if
the general purpose PAC gives a candidate a contribution of $100 or more, then the prohibition
in Section 85500(c) will apply to both the general purpose PAC and the independent expenditure
committee.
